etiquetas: cyberpunk, dystopia, industrial, science fiction, existentialism, transhumanism, machinery, entropy, survival, post-apocalyptic
titulo: TAIRRA Chronicles of a Broken World
portada: Un horizonte industrial cóncavo sumido en una penumbra verdosa, donde colosales engranajes superiores gotean hidrocarburo sobre una línea de siluetas humanas armadas con hierro.
disclaimer: This work is a manifestation of pure planetary physical and sociological causality, crafted with Artificial Intelligence.
prologo:
Rotation Cycle 84,092 began with an anomaly in the hydraulic compression sector. Inside Tairra, time does not pass through the movement of stars or the decay of isotopes, but through the metallic screeching of expansion joints that adjust the atmosphere according to the demands of the upper levels. An increase of 0.4 kilopascals in barometric pressure was enough to seal the fate of the inhabitants of the Lower Stratum: the capillaries in their lungs, adapted to a lower air density, gave way under the weight of physics dictated by pistons that never rest.

In corridors coated with synthetic slag, survival is an equation of friction. The biological organisms present here have learned to ignore the acrid smell of hydrogenated lubricant percolating from the levels above, seeping through cracks in the structure like a viscous rain that redefines the genetic code of those who dare to drink from the leaks. Will, in Tairra, is a disposable parameter; it is one more piece in the iron architecture, subject to the same fatigue tolerances as the tempered steel that supports the artificial sky.

Here, existence is tallied in the wear of joints and the gradual loss of synaptic integrity in the face of the constant hum of the master engines. The inhabitants do not live; they operate within a logical sequence that admits no exceptions. Death is not a transcendental event, but a technical failure, an interruption in the constant flow of machinery that, indifferent to the consciousness inhabiting its gears, continues its relentless rotation, calculating the destiny of every cell through the cold and absolute mathematics of metal.

capitulos.titulo: Chapter 1: The Last Shift at Orod
capitulos.sinopsis: Marbo calculates the peaks of industrial entropy on the southern front while the Ceiling's machinery begins to fail.
texto: The rattling of pneumatic riveters against the tempered steel of the Foundation Plates is not a sound, but a frequency of torture that dictates the biological pulse of the southern front. Beneath the dying, flickering glow of mercury vapor lamps, whose quartz lenses have been clouded by decades of ferrous dust, the workers move like mechanical shadows. Their pores, saturated with an impure mixture of industrial soot and saline secretions, have become impenetrable, transforming their epidermises into surfaces of tanned leather that can barely distinguish the temperature of the surrounding atmosphere. The patina of grime covering their bare torsos glitters with a toxic iridescence under the green light, giving them the appearance of foundry statues in the midst of erosion.

Isolated from the rhythmic frenzy of the assembly line, Marbo leans against a load-bearing beam that vibrates with a discordant frequency. His eyes, deeply sunken in their sockets due to chronic insomnia bordering on psychotic dissolution, scan the void with an oxidized brass astrolabe whose lenses have lost their original polish. His fingers, calloused and stained with an indelible blackness, trace invisible geometric paths in the air, calculating the divergence between the movement of the master gears and the resistance of the anchor bolts. For Marbo, the environment is not a place, but a series of algorithmic variables that he must decode while listening, with terrifying precision, to the rhythmic and constant dripping of hydraulic fluid leaking from the ceiling of the megastructure.

Every drop that strikes the ventilation grates is registered in his consciousness as an imminent failure, one more data point in the infinite archive of his memory. Marbo seeks neither salvation nor rebellion; his existence is contingent upon the recording of entropy. While the workers pound the metal until it becomes a plastic and malleable mass, he dissects reality with his calculations, archiving the sound of decay. His fingers trace the etchings of the astrolabe, feeling the friction of metal against skin, while his brain processes the structural wear of Tairra like a symphony of numbers collapsing upon themselves.

The fluid, a high-density synthetic compound intended to keep the upper gears in a state of perpetual lubrication, now falls in intermittent cascades that stain the drainage planes with a putrid viscosity. Marbo watches as the liquid, a mixture of heavy hydrocarbons and cooling additives, pools in the puddles of the lower platform, reacting with heavy metals to generate small clouds of corrosive vapor. He knows, from the reading of his own mental calculations, that this increase in seepage is not random waste; it is the prelude to a collapse in the expansion joints, a physical manifestation that Tairra's internal pressure is exceeding the tolerances of its own architecture.

However, the sector administration, installed in a hierarchy of willful blindness and blind bureaucracy, dismisses the numerical increase in leaks under the pretext that they are mere thermal fluctuations. To them, Marbo's chronic migraines are nothing more than the sign of a worker who has exceeded his service limit, a defective part whose subjectivity has begun to corrupt his operational functionality. The military foreman of the southern front, an individual whose limbs have been partially replaced by low-budget pneumatic prosthetics, ignores his warnings with a dismissive gesture, urging him to return to the metal-chipping in the exposed faults of the subsoil where the steel becomes brittle under the weight of the upper levels.

The workers, trapped in the inertia of a survival that admits no pauses, simply bow their heads, accepting the imposition as one accepts gravity. With rags impregnated with the same grease that consumes them, they clean their tools again and again, a futile ritual of maintenance that does not stop the corrosion, but provides them with an illusion of control in the face of their environment. Their hands, trembling from exhaustion and systemic metal poisoning, slide over the iron with a resignation that is, in itself, a form of slow death. Marbo watches them, recognizing in each of them the same physical degradation he feels coursing through his own nerves like a static current.

In the forge workshop, a zone where residual heat from the energy cores turns the air into a blast-furnace breath, the pack oxen remain immobilized. These animals, organisms genetically modified to withstand the heavy load of wet leather harnesses, bite their tethers with an agonizing heaviness, moving their jaws in a rhythm that mimics the heartbeat of the nearby hydraulic pistons. The soldiers tasked with patrolling the sector dedicate themselves with mechanical meticulousness to cleaning their corroded chainmail, removing the crust of rust that the saline and acidic atmosphere deposits on the tempered metal links. Each movement is an echo of the greater machinery: polishing, friction, wear, repetition.

The smell of hydrocarbons, a dense and penetrating mixture of sulfur, burnt oil, and ozone, permeates every corner of the structure, seeping through the pores of clothing and settling in the alveoli of those who inhabit the Lower Stratum. It is an aroma that announces the constant presence of the machine, a perfume of servitude that marks every living being as property of the architecture. Marbo, feeling a sharp pang in his right temple—an electric shock racing along his trigeminal nerve with the fury of a short circuit—extracts a minimal dose of opium extract from a fold in his uniform. The bitterness of the compound is barely perceptible against the nausea caused by the saturated atmosphere, but the effect, though brief, is sufficient to dampen the din.

Every sound of the machinery, from the grinding of rotating shafts to the hiss of released steam, is etched into his memory with painful sharpness. His senses have become high-fidelity measuring instruments, incapable of filtering out unnecessary noise or ignoring the underlying structure. The impossibility of blinking is total, not due to a physiological issue, but from the fear that a single millisecond of blindness might be the moment when the physics of Tairra finally decides to break its fragile equilibrium. He remains there, static, processing a reality that others evade, becoming the observer of a collapse that only he understands in its full mathematical extent.

Suddenly, the Ceiling emits a dull creak, a low-frequency harmonic vibration that passes through the metal plates and resonates in the bone cavities of all those present. It is not a sound produced by movement, but by the extreme fatigue of the material, the agonizing moan of millions of tons of iron attempting to support an atmospheric pressure they can no longer contain. The tools hanging on the walls of the southern front vibrate in unison, producing a dissonant musical note that highlights the precariousness of the structure. The tremor lasts no longer than a sigh, but it is enough for the inertia of the labor to break.

The workers stop their tasks simultaneously, as if they had been stripped of their electrical commands. For a second, an absolute silence descends upon the sector, a silence that is more terrifying than the constant noise of the machines. All eyes, clouded by fatigue and soot, rise toward the artificial sky, seeking answers in the network of pipes and beams that are lost in the upper mist. Up there, in the shadows of the iron dome, the machinery begins to turn again, but its rhythm has changed: the beat of the hydraulic marches is erratic, losing the mathematical cadence that allowed for the existence of this ecosystem.

Marbo puts away his astrolabe with a sharp movement, feeling how the cold of the metal transfers to his hands. His figures, those he has been accumulating shift after shift, reorder themselves in his mind to confirm the truth he had just visualized: the entropy has finally crossed the safety threshold. The terrarium is not just an architecture of iron, it is a trap that has just engaged its locking mechanisms. He does not utter a single word, for he knows that the voice is insufficient in the face of collapse, but his eyes reflect the certainty of the end: the implacable mathematics of Tairra have stopped counting time to begin tallying the downfall, and on the southern front, the last shift has just reached its point of total saturation.

capitulos.sinopsis: The Sunken Sector of Orod suffers a total disconnection of its stellar spotlights and a deluge of industrial sludge.
texto: The immense stellar spotlights embedded in the roof's dome, those that simulated a celestial vault for the Sunken Sector of Orod, began to sway in an erratic choreography. The power supply frequency fluctuated with an electrical violence that threatened to tear the very fabric of the confined space; discharge diodes, the size of small buildings, spat white-blue arc flashes that left scars in the air and cast spasmodic, gargantuan, and distorted shadows across the scrap plains. Each flicker was a syncopation in Tairra's heartbeat, a reminder that light was not a solar right, but a voltage privilege managed by a central hub indifferent to the base's needs.

The hum of static, a low frequency that used to be background noise imperceptible to the inhabitants' lungs, escalated into a deafening roar that stifled any attempt at human communication. The machinery hidden within Orod's walls began to groan, a metallic sound of material fatigue, until, with a sharp and definitive crack that resonated in every being's chest like mechanical thunder, the master circuit shattered. Absolute darkness, a blackness so dense it possessed texture and weight, engulfed the sector; only the residual glow of metallic alloys remained after a few seconds of retinal adaptation, a faint and spectral glimmer that barely outlined the contours of a world destined for technical obsolescence.

The temperature, deprived of the radiant heat from the stellar spotlights, suffered a dizzying thermal collapse, plunging toward the absolute zero of survival. The air, already saturated with humidity and iron particles, began to crystallize in a geometric choreography; vapor molecules transformed into needles of frost that deposited themselves with surgical precision upon exposed surfaces. The sentinels, statues of flesh and galvanized scrap guarding the filtration hatches, watched as their protective layers turned rigid, their armors becoming cold shells that creaked with every spasm of muscles numbed by the sudden artificial winter.

In the heart of the megastructure, the master gears managing artificial gravity lost their synchronization, unable to compensate for the system's inertia when deprived of the constant flow of data from the compression sector. A dull roar announced the disaster: industrial seals, fatigued by decades of incessant pressure, burst as if they were fragile membranes of cellophane. A viscous mass of oily rain, a denigrating mixture of refined soot, synthetic lubricants, and organic waste fluids from the upper levels, began to cascade, a black cataract that buried any attempt at order under a deluge of industrial waste.

The sludge, a petroleum-colored amalgam distilling the poison of centuries of inefficiency, slammed into the lower districts with the force of an avalanche. Orod's streets, originally laid out with a drainage logic that no longer served, became evacuation channels for the metropolis's waste. Supply warehouses, where inhabitants accumulated their reserves of filtered oxygen and polymer rations, were the first to be reclaimed by the flood, the cargo structures dissolving before the relentless advance of the dark tide rising with a geological and lethal cadence.

In the desperation of the collapse, mechanical alarms, fueled by the meager residual steam pressure still circulating through secondary pipes, howled in the darkness with an agonizing sound, a rhythmic lament that marked the time of extinction. The stridency of the sirens, mixed with the hissing of bursting valves, created a cacophonous atmosphere where the cries of the population were barely discernible; citizens, prey to a flight instinct as primary as the physics condemning them, began a stampede toward the upper platforms, climbing service ladders and rusted structures that groaned under the weight of human hopelessness.

The chaos turned into tragedy when high-pressure thermal ducts, bursting from the abrupt contraction of materials, released boiling oil over the plazas. The steaming liquid, capable of melting synthetic clothing fibers and flesh itself, bathed the improvised tents where families tried to protect themselves from the cold, turning the refuge into a pyre of melted plastic and toxic smoke. The air, saturated with combustion vapors and the sweet stench of burning organic components, became unbreathable, forcing survivors to risk climbing toward the highest point, where the atmospheric pressure was a torture for the eardrums, but the sludge had not yet reached.

Marbo, trapped in his cabinet, heard the horror with the clarity of someone unable to look away from an equation with no solution. Each cry of pain, each dull detonation of pipes bursting on the level below, was etched into his memory through the vibration of the metallic walls he felt through his hands. His own existence, reduced to a series of electrical impulses and survival probability calculations, seemed to crumble along with the architecture surrounding him; he memorized the acoustic pattern of death, feeling how Tairra's logic turned against its occupants, proving that they were nothing more than minor errors in the execution of an eternal program.

From his elevated position near Orod's perimeter wall, the marshy plain revealed itself as an infinite abyss, an expanse of shadows and craters where reality had been obliterated. Beneath the dead sky, where the comforting glow of the spotlights had once existed, only a black void stretched, devouring the depths, an ocean of debris and oil where life had ceased to be a relevant variable. The southern military front, a bastion of technological order, had been wiped off the map; in its place now stretched a frozen industrial mire, a static cemetery where the remnants of civilization froze under the relentless rain from the upper levels.

Marbo's solitude became absolute as he understood that no external intervention would come to mitigate the damage; the megastructure's gears were simply adjusting to a new configuration, one that did not include biological presence in the Sunken Sector. The system, in its infinite coldness, considered Orod a dispensable section, a rounding error in the global level's energy expenditure. He watched, with a lucidity tinged with terror, as the last vestiges of illumination on the horizon flickered out one by one, confirming that the disconnection was not an incident, but a deliberate process of purging in the infinite cycle of Tairra.

capitulos.sinopsis: Dastael transports the wounded soldiers to Lein’s field hospital, where somatic empathy manifests.
texto: The field hospital of Lein was nothing more than a biological intrusion into the cold geometry of Tairra, a receptacle of rotting yew wood that creaked to the rhythm of the master pistons. At the center of this ephemeral dwelling, Lein lit a candle of animal fat, whose flickering flame cast long, grotesque shadows against the support beams. The air in the pit was an unbreathable mixture of saltpeter and the oily viscosity that seeped from the joints above, a metallic stench that clung to the throat like a scab. Her linen habit, originally immaculate, now presented a cartography of dark stains, an amalgam of industrial mud and ferrous residue that marked her futile struggle against the decay of the environment.

All around her, agony manifested in a horizontal array of broken bodies. The wounded, piled upon wool blankets that exuded a perennial dampness, exhaled arrhythmic noises, a compendium of gasps that barely managed to overcome the monotonous hum of the megastructure. The smell of burnt flesh, the product of the corrosive interaction between human skin and the gear oil poured from the fissures in the ceiling, saturated the room, creating a thick, almost liquid atmosphere where life was reduced to the mere persistence of pain before the inevitable systemic failure of the organism.

The hospital entrance was violated by the figure of Dastael, whose silhouette seemed sculpted from the same metallic slag that coated the walls of Tairra. His steps, rigid and laden with the inertia of a machine, shook the muddy ground beneath his feet. Dastael did not walk with the lightness of the organic, but with the calculated displacement of a brute-force vector; on his shoulders, the weight of three dying soldiers was merely a load variable that did not alter his center of gravity. His armor of rusted iron plates, turned into a shroud of thick mud, clinked with every impact, a dry sound that resonated with the violence of a technical sentence.

Lein rushed toward the newcomer, her hands, calloused by constant contact with bandages and ointments, reached out with an almost frantic urgency. Upon reaching the first of the soldiers, whose torso had been charred by a discharge of overheated synthetic lubricant, her fingers sank into the living, cauterized flesh. In that instant, physical contact transcended medicine to become a forbidden neural bridge; Lein’s consciousness was invaded, violated by the imprint of an agony that did not belong to her but which, before the logic of Tairra, claimed its right to be felt. Her eyes opened wide, bulging, while somatic terror coursed through her marrow like a high-voltage current.

The organism's response was immediate, a chain reaction that ignored any protocol of free will. Lein’s skin, in areas symmetrical to the soldier’s wounds, began to inflame with unnatural speed; blisters of a whitish liquid sprouted on her forearms and cheeks, bursting into a cascade of cellular fluid. The atrocious pain of the chemical burn replicated in her nervous system with terrifying fidelity. The priestess collapsed onto the mud, her vocal cords emitting a torn scream that was lost in the din of the master engines, her body convulsing under the weight of an empathy that functioned as a death sentence.

Dastael, standing over her, contemplated the collapse with a paralysis that emanated from his own neurological configuration. His military analgesia, a chemical shielding that command had implanted to ensure combat efficiency, prevented him from processing the physics of another’s suffering; to him, Lein was not suffering, she was failing in her performance, displaying an operational instability that he found unintelligible. His internal sensors registered the frequency of the screams as simple sound wave oscillations, devoid of emotional meaning. However, his hyper-vigilance—programmed to prioritize the integrity of assets—dictated a single response: correct the position of the fallen subject.

With an execution devoid of any hint of tactile delicacy, Dastael bent down, his metallic joints emitting a sharp screech of friction. He grabbed Lein by the arms; his hands, wrapped in worn bandages, squeezed with a force that threatened to splinter the priestess’s bones. He lifted her with the mechanical indifference used to manipulate a replaceable component, holding her aloft as if she were an iron pillar destined to support the hospital ceiling. She, disoriented and bathed in cold sweat, allowed herself to be held while her nervous system tried, in vain, to recalibrate the levels of trauma.

On the threshold of the hospital, the presence of Marbo altered the static dynamics of the scene. He entered with a wavering step, dragging a bundle of charred parchment that gave off dense smoke, remains of a library that the structure had decided to consume. His eyes, clouded by soot, met the scene of horror: the suffering priestess, held by the automaton of flesh and iron that was Dastael, whose rigid posture defied gravity and ethics. The tension between them was a latent electrical charge, a psychological friction that exceeded even the roar of the external gears.

In the darkest corner, where the light from the fat candle barely managed to penetrate the gloom, Bashole remained like a statuette of silence. His hands moved with a hypnotic cadence, cleaning the string of his compound bow, the friction of the fabric against the taut fiber producing a subtle whistle. His eyes, cold and analytical, scanned each of Dastael’s movements and the physical degradation of Lein. Bashole did not intervene; to him, the scene was a simple record of variables, one more observation in the endless sequence of attrition that defined survival within the bowels of Tairra. The hospital, far from being a refuge, was now a collision node where flesh desperately tried to reconcile itself with the insensitivity of metal.

capitulos.sinopsis: Bashole and Dastael defend the lower conduits from the attack of the first Noise-Crystallized.
texto: The hydraulic compression sector in the lower levels of Tairra began to emit a dissonant hum, a low-intensity frequency that the inhabitants of the lower strata initially mistook for the cyclic readjustment of the master pistons. However, the sound lacked the mathematical cadence required for a maintenance function; it was an erratic screech, an acoustic distortion that made the stagnant lubricant in the foundry grates vibrate. A tide of oily sludge, laden with synthetic detritus and remnants of decomposed organic matter, began to boil, revealing bubbles that burst with a metallic snap. The splashing, improper for the standard gravity of the channels, echoed in the total gloom like a broken march, announcing that the city's ecosystem was beginning to be invaded by an external anomaly.

The rearguard patrols, composed of sentinels whose existence was limited to sealing leaks and maintaining the integrity of the corridors, retreated, prey to a visceral terror. Their helmet sensors, designed to detect variations in atmospheric pressure, flooded their visors with red geometric interference alerts. It was then that the shadows fractured to give way to the Noise-Crystallized. They emerged from the flooded channels with impossible kinematics, moving their asymmetrical chassis of cast iron and grey silicon crusts that crackled with residual static. Their limbs, chaotically welded onto unstable energy cores, dragged remnants of pipes and gears, transforming the architecture of the environment into an extension of their own aberrant anatomy.

Bashole, perched on a rusted iron beam that hung over the main channel like an exposed rib of the megastructure, observed the advance with surgical coldness. The archer of stillness, whose presence seemed an anomaly in the reigning chaos, adjusted the folds of his green hood, a relic of synthetic fiber now torn and frayed. His eyes, hardened by decades of exposure to industrial vacuum and the toxic glow of plasma leaks, fixed with mathematical precision on the creatures' joints. In his mind, each Crystallized shed its grotesque form to become a diagram of stresses and mechanical weaknesses, a target of critical points waiting to be executed by the physics of impact.

Bashole's technical prowess was activated through a series of mental protocols deeply rooted in his procedural memory. He froze his breath in the icy air of the stratum, eliminating the gaseous exchange that might disturb the stability of his torso. He paced the movement of his limbs with the slow beat of his heart, reducing his heart rate until each muscle contraction became a predictable maneuver. His hands, which moments before trembled due to chronic fatigue and exposure to the upper level's vapors, became rigid as yew, acquiring a mineral immobility characteristic of the foundations of Tairra itself. The environment transformed into a logical sequence where only the tension of the string and the trajectory of the force vector existed.

The string of the Bone Composite Bow, a piece carved from the fossilized remains of a former era, released with a dry mechanical response that cut through the thick air like a blade. The sound was a sharp click, a discordant note in the roar of the engines. The arrow, whose tip was forged with fragments recovered from the foundation iron, traversed the space with a perfect ballistic trajectory, impacting directly on the hydraulic joint that maintained the structural coherence of the first Crystallized. The construct convulsed, its cast-iron chassis releasing a trail of violet sparks before collapsing onto the oily mud, its physical integrity dissolved in a dance of disarticulated gears that sank into the mire.

Simultaneously, Dastael executed a downward maneuver from the upper catwalk, a jump that defied the inertia of the structure. The warrior unleashed his two-handed greatsword, a weapon of tempered steel whose mass exceeded the weight standards recommended for his build, directly onto the skull of another Crystallized. The impact resonated with superhuman intensity, releasing a discharge of grey static that ionized the surrounding air and made the hair of everyone present on the catwalk stand on end. The creature fragmented under the blow, its grey crusts flying in all directions, while Dastael landed with impassive stability, keeping the edge of his weapon in direct contact with the ground, dissipating the remaining energy into the sector's discharge grid.

On the evacuation line, Lein was struck by the metaphysical shockwave that the creatures emitted upon dying. He fell to the ground, feeling a reflex headache, an unbearable pressure behind his eyes, threaten to disconnect his cognitive functions. The pain was the direct result of the collision between his biology and the disturbing frequency emanating from the crystallized remains. His faith, a rudimentary but tenacious mental construct, acted as a forced dam in his consciousness, preventing the collapse from being total. Clinging to the cold metallic surface of the floor, Lein forced his synapses to maintain the flow, repeating a logical survival sequence while the world around him oscillated between the grey of reality and the white of absolute static.

Bashole, without losing the rhythm of his controlled breathing, took down the last attackers of the vanguard with a series of rapid shots that lacked apparent effort. Each arrow found a joint or an energy port, dismantling the creatures before they could reach the defensive position. As he lowered the bow, the ocular hyper-concentration that had kept his nervous system in a steel web began to dissipate, letting the effect of adrenaline and traumatic effort take control. A chronic and uncontrollable tremor returned to shake his calloused hands, reminding him that, in Tairra, no victory was free and that the human body is always the variable that exhausts itself fastest before the persistence of the eternal machinery.

The group managed to hold the position on the bridge, a narrow service passage that served as the final line of defense before the main ventilation ducts. Silence returned, not as an absence of sound, but as the return to the monotonous hum of the city's gears. The corpses of the Crystallized lay on the platform, being slowly absorbed by the oil seepage, as if the megastructure itself were reclaiming the strange matter that dared to corrupt its order. Bashole leaned against the railing, feeling the metal vibrate under his back, conscious that the Noise-Crystallized were but the omen of a failure in the great equation; a warning that the first vector had impacted, but that many more were already on their way through the circuits of Tairra.

capitulos.sinopsis: Marbo demonstrates the biological impossibility of remaining in Orod and maps out the route to the Fifth Crystal.
texto: The air in Orod’s abandoned headquarters possessed the texture of rusted metal and the density of an irrevocable judicial sentence. Marbo unrolled the scroll, a sheet of synthetic leather tanned by exposure to acidic vapors and speckled with chemical burns that resembled constellations of a forbidden geography. As he spread it across the tempered steel table, the resulting sound was a dry snap, like the cracking of a tendon under extreme tension. A tallow candle, its flame oscillating with an erratic rhythm caused by drafts filtering through expansion joints, cast long, distorted shadows over the geometric traces that defined the internal structure of Tairra.

Military officers, survivors of a chain of command that had ceased to exist cycles ago, surrounded the table with a rigidity unnatural for men who were still breathing. Their faces, marked by soot and the chronic fatigue of prolonged vigils, were intermittently illuminated by the dying glow of the candle. Each of them, hands gloved in leather hardened by incessant contact with rust, rubbed them vigorously, seeking a warmth the environment systematically denied them. In the upper chambers of the Sunken Sector, the cold was not a simple absence of energy, but a physical entity, a cryogenic pressure that pierced the armor plating to crystallize residual moisture in the lungs of those who still dared to stand upright.

Marbo did not seem to feel the icy caress of the atmosphere or the hostility of the gazes that, however skeptical, pierced the back of his neck with the intensity of beams from a fusion furnace. His hand, steady despite the systemic weakness gripping every biological being on that level, took up an analog compass, a precision tool whose pivots had been lubricated with synthetic oil from the upper levels. With surgical parsimony, he began to trace inverse thermal curves onto the parchment, linking the data gathered during the recent blackout of the Ceiling’s static floodlights. The artificial light that once guaranteed the circadian cycle had extinguished, leaving behind a trail of atmospheric imbalance that Marbo translated into vectors of entropy and structural collapse.

In his mind, the anomaly of absolute hypermnesia processed the environment with the coldness of a central processor designed for the autopsy of worlds. There was no room for speculation or hope; every centimeter of the data sheet, every fracture in the refrigerant gas pressure, converged into a single logical conclusion. His fingers, agile over the markings, recalculated the decomposition rate of the moss paste accumulated in the Orod silos under the increasing water saturation invading the levels. The air, dense with excess vapors, not only stifled the alveoli but catalyzed the degradation of any organic sustenance with terrifying mathematical efficiency.

“Twenty-one days,” Marbo decreed, his voice, devoid of emotional inflection, cutting through the silence like a diamond blade. His eyes, fixed on the convergence of the lines that predicted the end of biological viability in the sector, did not seek the validation of his companions. “Not a single thermal overlap more. Water saturation is not an environmental variable; it is a constant condition. In three weeks, the nutritional mass will be a broth of ferrous pathogens, and the lungs of those who dwell here will collapse against the density of the waterlogged air. The physics of Tairra allows for no extensions.” His pronouncement echoed against the sheet-metal walls, stripping the headquarters of any scrap of false security.

Lein, sitting on a discarded ammunition crate, watched Marbo with eyes clouded by an anguish that transcended her own consciousness. Between her fingers, inflamed and cracked from constant exposure to the sector’s chemicals, she clutched a secular iron icon, a piece of sacred scrap metal representing the remnant of a forgotten faith. Lein possessed a somatic empathy that acted like a poorly tuned radio receiver; she perceived the officers’ fear not as an idea, but as a physical weight on her sternum, a numbness that prevented her from moving. The group’s collective desperation, fueled by Marbo’s certainty, manifested in her as an electrical oppression coursing through her nerves, a silent torture born of her capacity to feel the imminent cessation of life in her comrades.

Behind Marbo’s chair, Dastael remained in a posture so perfect and devoid of life that he could well have been a statue sculpted in the foundry of the lower levels. His eyes, hidden behind a polarized glass visor, did not blink; his auditory sensors filtered out the sound of the officers’ labored breathing to focus exclusively on tactical analysis. To Dastael, fear was irrelevant data, a distraction that diminished efficiency in movement logistics. His mind functioned as a war automaton, evaluating hull resistance, friction levels in the service corridors, and the probability of success for a transit toward the middle regions. He felt no pity for the condemned, but he counted them as assets that had to be moved if the group’s coherence was to be maintained.

The silence that followed Marbo’s revelation was populated only by the electrical hum of the failing ceiling lights and the rhythmic dripping of lubricant seeping from the pipes above. The officers, men who had watched their families succumb to the collapse of the levels without shedding a tear, felt the snapping of their resolve for the first time. Marbo’s logic, implacable and devoid of consolation, had become the only law in effect in Tairra. They had survived the wear of steel, but the mathematics of time, that countdown of three weeks before the air itself turned into poison, was a force against which there was no armor.

Marbo raised his index finger, covered in black soot, and placed it with precision on the edge of the middle regions on the map. Under the flickering glow of the candle, the point indicating the Fifth Crystal of Kaftakon revealed itself as a spot of technical clarity, a substructure that still retained residual energy. The Crystal was not a myth, but the only circuit breaker capable of reversing the sludge flood suffocating the foundations of their existence. It was a beacon of ancient technology, an enclave capable of purifying the atmosphere and diverting the water flow toward Tairra’s main drains, providing the only margin of technical maneuverability against extinction.

One by one, the officers approached the metal table. There were no speeches, no promises of glory, no invocations of hope; pragmatism was the only currency in that abyss. With trembling hands, yet firm in their purpose, they stamped their seals and signatures onto the deployment documents. The order for mass evacuation was ratified by the same cold logic that dictated their deaths. The March of the Dispossessed, an exodus through mechanical corridors and high-pressure tunnels, was officially marked beneath the iron vault of a world that, indifferent to their struggle, continued its eternal and mechanical cycle of rotation.

capitulos.titulo: Chapter 6: The Exit through the Fault
capitulos.sinopsis: The great column of the dispossessed abandons the ruins of the city and begins its journey through the central swamp.
texto: The departure from Orod’s Sunken Sector was not an act of desperation, but an evacuation of exhausted parameters. An immense and miserable column, composed of an amalgam of emaciated civilians and soldiers whose prosthetics screeched under the weight of rust, began to pass through the hydraulic gates that had once served as the border between civilization and the abyss. The air emanating from the fault held a density that was almost solid, saturated with coolant vapors and suspended metallic particles. Upon their shoulders, these beings carried rudimentary bundles tied with synthetic fiber cables, containing the meager remnants of a life measured in cycles of use, while they pushed cargo carts whose iron axles, deprived of lubrication, emitted harmonic laments that pierced the sterile silence of the tunnels.

The floor at the threshold of the fault was a viscous trap composed of hydrocarbon sludge, a residue accumulated over decades of industrial runoff. The beasts of burden, genetically modified animals whose hooves had been replaced by defective hydraulic traction platforms, slipped incessantly, unable to find purchase on the oily surface. Their flanks heaved with an erratic rhythm as mud seeped into their limb actuators, causing them to sink to their knees in the chemical blackness. The soldiers, their faces covered by filtration masks that barely managed to sift the acrid air, were forced to abandon their weapons to brace their shoulders against the rotten wooden wheels, pushing with desperate force to prevent the vehicles from becoming tombstones submerged in the dense mire.

The effort was a choreography of muscles strained to the point of tearing and gears that protested with every inch gained. There was no room for compassion, only for the calculation of inertia; if a cart stopped, the entire column risked collapsing under the weight of barometric pressure, which in that transit zone fluctuated with seismic violence. Desperation did not manifest in shouts, but in the absolute silence of those who feared that an excess of oxygen in their lungs might accelerate their own degradation. Each meter conquered toward the outside was a victory against entropy, a momentary triumph over a structure that, by design, sought to compress them until they were reduced to solid waste, easy for the automated maintenance systems to sweep away.

Before definitively abandoning the threshold, the citizens paused for an instant to look back, a redundant biological gesture that defied their status as expendable parts. The colossal industrial towers of Orod rose behind them like specters of iron and shadow, ghostly profiles lost in an oily fog so thick it erased the very notion of height. The Ceiling’s floodlights, once artificial suns that dictated the metropolis’s work rhythm, remained extinguished, darkened by a fault in the master circuit, leaving the city to submerge into an absolute darkness, interrupted only by the brief sparks of relays still dying in the depths. It was a sight of mechanical desolation: the architecture, indifferent to their flight, had already begun to reclaim the empty space, closing its shutters as if nothing had ever been there.

Dastael led the march, becoming the axis upon which the fate of the dispossessed pivoted. His gait was an affront to fatigue; he advanced with monotonous rigidity, a human metronome moving over the superficial metallic foundation of the fault without his soles slipping. He carried the heavy tool chests, containers of tungsten alloys that would have shattered the shoulders of any ordinary man, but in his case, the burden seemed integrated into his own skeletal structure. There was not a single sign of exhaustion on his face; his features, expressionless as tempered steel, maintained a mathematical concentration on the terrain, always seeking the line of greatest structural stability to guide the mass of people who, blindly, followed his footsteps as if he were an extension of the master code.

Every stride of Dastael was a calculation of precision, a resource management where the wear of his own flesh was the price to pay for the group’s advance. His consciousness seemed to have detached itself from any feeling of anguish, becoming an extension of the topographic maps he consulted internally. He walked on the rusted metal as if he knew every pore of the plate, every manufacturing defect that might give way under the weight of the convoy. The refugees, seeing him move with such mechanical efficiency, felt an artificial security that calmed their panic, allowing them to continue the march toward the central swamp, where reality became even more hostile and physics no longer dictated the order of the city, but the chaos of environmental decomposition.

Lein, however, represented the most vulnerable link in that chain. She walked among the rows of the elderly, those whose wear variables already exceeded any limit of operability, holding the arm of a woman who shivered violently due to the thermal gradient of the exit. The cold of the swamp, laden with corrosive humidity, acted as a catalyst in the health of the dispossessed. As soon as Lein felt the first spasm of the elderly woman, her body began to react with an involuntary and dangerous empathy: her lungs began to collapse in unison with hers, somatizing the respiratory cooling. The priestess, accustomed to absorbing the pain of others like a fault in her own processing system, fought to remain calm while the freezing air, impregnated with traces of acid, burned her throat.

Lein’s struggle was an internal battle against a biology that refused to be a simple machine for processing sensations. While holding the elderly woman, she tried to redirect the thermal flow of her organism toward the woman’s extremities, a process that required an unwavering will and left her own systems on the verge of collapse. It was a precarious balance between compassion and functional survival. With every step, she felt the fatigue of a thousand different lives converging in her nervous system, a burden that not even the processors of the higher levels would have been able to index without suffering a catastrophic overload. Faith, for Lein, was simply another form of preventive maintenance, an attempt to prevent the soul from fragmenting before the immensity of the abandonment.

Apart from the mass, Bashole advanced along the higher mud ridges, maintaining a tactical distance necessary for observation. His Bone Compound Bow, a piece of rudimentary but lethal ballistic technology, was wrapped in layers of synthetic insulation and treated leather to prevent the humidity of the swamp from destroying the dry tendon tensors. Bashole did not look ahead like Dastael, nor to the sides like Lein; his eyes scanned the horizon of slag in search of biological predators or terrain flaws that could compromise the safety of the column. He moved with an unnatural lightness, almost as if his center of gravity were adjusted so as not to disturb even a single mote of the thick mist that was beginning to envelop them.

Every fiber of his being was dedicated to the protection of the group, even if his methods were as cold as the environment surrounding them. If a bow’s tensor loosened due to the pressure, Bashole would stop for a second, with surgical precision, to readjust the tension, demonstrating a discipline that admitted no room for human error. In the swamp, survival was a variable that was adjusted with arrows of bone and nerve, tools that had to be kept in perfect operating condition. Bashole was the silent vigilante of an exodus that lacked hope, but still possessed a logic of persistence; he knew the bow was not just a weapon, but the last guarantee against the chaos that inhabited the space outside the walls of Tairra.

Closing the rearguard, Marbo processed reality through an altered lens. He consumed a regulated dose of synthetic opium to filter the burst of information produced in his temples as he attempted to track the exact position of each of the seven hundred civilians who made up the column. His eyes, injected with a neon red due to the exertion of the neurological implants, constantly scanned the vital signs, the distances, and the march patterns of each individual, ensuring that no one was left behind in the mud of the outer sector. For Marbo, the column was not a group of human beings, but a massive set of moving data, a dynamic equation that had to be kept within tolerance parameters to avoid the total collapse of the group.

The opium, in this context, was a tool of technical stabilization. Without it, the white noise of collective desperation, the fear of the elderly, and the weariness of the soldiers would filter directly into his thought centers, causing a failure in his command judgment. Marbo, operating on the threshold of dissociation, registered every halt, every stumble, and every fall, constantly recalculating escape routes as the column moved, step by step, into the gray immensity of the central swamp. The security of the mechanical walls of Orod was now just a blurred memory in the group’s cache memory, an architecture that dissolved behind them as they advanced toward the absolute nothingness.

The March of the Dispossessed had formally begun. Beneath the dome of Tairra, which stretched over them like a vault of eternal misfortune, the column was nothing more than a line of biological pixels moving over a surface of rust and chemicals. The air became increasingly heavy, laden with the metallic smell of coolant leaks that fell from the Ceiling like a rain of slow death. There was no going back; the Sunken Sector of Orod had been sealed by the very weight of inertia, and the refugees, now abandoned to the mercy of the implacable laws of the swamp’s physics, continued their advance. They were, in that moment, a living structure attempting to resist the logic of the system, an error that kept walking in a world that only understood the language of metal and the definitive fault.

capitulos.sinopsis: The collapse of a wagon exposes the ferrous infrastructure of Tairra and reveals the Foundation Plates.
texto: The column of refugees crawled across the plain like a mechanized parasite upon an open wound, trudging over a vast layer of crushed rock and organic sediment that disintegrated beneath their feet. The ground was no solid earth, but an accumulation of millennia of debris, industrial ash, and sludge saturated with synthetic lubricant exuding from the hidden entrails of Tairra. Every step taken by the survivors was an exercise in desperation, a calculated sinking into a swampy mass that, in its grayish viscosity, seemed to be trying to reclaim the bones of those who dared to profane its stillness. The wagons, laden with the last remnants of their existences, groaned under an overload that the wheels, already deformed by metal fatigue and the torsion of the terrain, could barely support in their futile attempt to remain afloat.

The air in that sector was heavy with a suffocating humidity, a mist that carried the metallic stench of the depths and clung to the skin like a film of filthy oil. Under the constant weight of the military wagons, the surface mud yielded with a repulsive suction sound, a clicking of a vacuum that marked the rhythm of a hopeless march. The draft animals, skeletal creatures whose milky eyes reflected the inertia of a life reduced to pulling power, sank their limbs up to the hock, creating a chaos of prints that were instantly erased by the constant infiltration of liquid sediment bubbling up from the subsoil.

In the center of the procession, destiny chose to manifest itself through a sudden structural failure. One of the supply wagons, loaded with ingots of recycled metal and filtration containers, suffered a critical fracture in its reinforced wooden axle, a component that could no longer absorb the asymmetric torsions of the uneven terrain. With a dry crack, similar to the snapping of a spine, the chassis buckled violently, spilling its load into the mud as the wooden structure was pulverized under the pressure. The inertia of the movement did not stop there; the earth, displaced by the weight, lost its integrity and opened like an abyssal wound. The wagon plummeted into the void with blinding speed, sinking thirty meters through layers of organic matter and strata of sediment, passing through centuries of accumulated debris until reaching, with a metallic thunder that made the air vibrate, a surface of ultra-dense, immovable, and lethally cold ferrous alloy.

Marbo, whose eyes, accustomed to the dim light of the maintenance chambers, detected the phenomenon before anyone else, approached the edge of the collapse with a caution tinged with horror. He knelt on the mud, ignoring the freezing sludge seeping through the seams of his worn clothing, and extended his hands, covered in scars and ink stains, toward the depths. With precise movements, he began to push aside the wet sediments that were still sliding toward the pit, revealing the nature of the surface where the wagon had ended its days. Beneath the shattered wagon there was no rock, no earth, not even the remains of the previous structure; there was a plain of metal polished by pressure, a material of an aberrant density that no human should ever have touched.

His fingers, trembling yet analytical, traced the surface of the plate, discovering a series of industrial assembly marks on a titanic scale, geometric recordings that defied human comprehension. These were the signatures of Azrriamoh, the mechanical demiurge who had forged the foundations of Tairra with a precision dictated by physical laws alien to biology. The swamp water, trapped by the absolute impermeability of the bottom plate, accumulated steadily at the bottom of the pit, creating a murky mirror that made natural drainage impossible. At that instant, a chilling revelation raced through Marbo's mind: the central sector was not solid ground, but an immense floating raft of perpetual mud suspended over a floor that did not belong to it. Everything they knew, all their history and their painful journey, was nothing more than a superficial layer of residue sliding over an impenetrable metal plate.

Dastael, observing Marbo's stillness and the wagon trapped in the depths, stepped into the pit without a second of hesitation, his boots splashing into the viscous mixture as he descended toward the abyss that had just opened. The giant did not seek explanations; his purpose was anchored to the survival of the group and the recovery of the resources. He hooked his thick arms, wrapped in bandages of hardened leather and slag, under the fractured axle of the wagon, feeling the weight of the vehicle attempt to dislocate his joints. With a grimace of controlled agony, he tensed the muscles of his back, ignoring the screeching of his own tendons reaching their limit and the chronic wear that gnawed at his skeleton.

Lein, remaining at the edge of the trench like a spectator forced to witness the fragility of his own flesh against the persistence of the metal, felt a stabbing pain in his own ankles, a sympathetic response to the inhuman effort Dastael exerted to lift the load. The sight of the giant's tendons, taut beneath the skin like steel cables about to snap, gave him unbearable vertigo. Without thinking, Lein began to shout technical instructions, correcting Dastael's posture through precise orders regarding support points and centers of gravity, aware that any miscalculation would result in an involuntary and definitive fracture for the man holding the fate of the load.

The tension in the pit was absolute; every gram of force exerted by Dastael was met by the inert resistance of the Azrriamoh plate. The soldiers of the group, seeing the precariousness of the situation, mobilized quickly to provide assistance, descending into the pit with wedges of Foundation Iron recovered from the upper levels. They placed the metal blocks, forged with the same alloys as the bottom plate, to shim the wheels of the wagon and prevent it from sinking further into the mud when Dastael achieved the necessary lift. The clinking of metal against metal echoed across the desolate plain like a dead mechanical echo, a sound that did not belong to organic life, but to a machinery that, despite its millennia of existence, did not know the concept of death.

After an effort that seemed to last for hours, fueled by the sheer desperation of those who inhabit a world that seeks to discard them, the wagon was finally extracted from the quagmire. Its structure, now more damaged and twisted than before, settled onto the improvised wedges on the revealed metal surface. The relief was not physical, but purely existential; seeing the metal beneath their feet, the refugees understood that the ground they had always walked upon was a lie, an accumulation of filth hiding the true iron framework of their artificial world. The column resumed its advance, but this time with a cautious slowness, each step resonating with a new coldness, aware now that they were walking, in essence, upon the cold and eternal skeleton of Tairra, a world whose logic had no room for compassion or doubt.

capitulos.sinopsis: Scarcity forces the civilians to consume the Moss Paste Ration, triggering a widespread biological rejection.
texto: The pulse of darkness descended upon the Roof of Tairra with the geometric punctuality of clockwork, plunging the corridors into a bluish twilight broken only by the erratic blinking of emergency lamps. In the lower levels, where gravity feels like an accumulated weight upon the spine, the column of displaced persons established a makeshift camp between the interstices of the great load-bearing pillars. The children, whose small hands still retained the agility necessary to navigate the reliefs of the mega-structure, spread out across the exposed metal plates. With dull steel knives, they began to scrape the dense, velvety crust of hydroponic moss that grew in the damp joints; it was mutant vegetation, fueled by coolant leaks and the condensed vapors of the thermal processing levels. The gathering was a dance of desperation, for every gram of that green mass represented the only barrier against imminent metabolic collapse, given that the synthetic emergency ration reserves had run out three rotation cycles ago, leaving behind only empty wrappers that crunched under the soldiers' boots.

In the center of the improvised camp, large iron cauldrons—relics forged in the era of primary construction—steamed over low-intensity fires fueled by polymeric waste. The military cooks, their hands calloused from handling abrasive materials, poured the crushed moss into the vessels, adding blocks of rancid animal fat collected in the clandestine slaughterhouses of sector fourteen. To mitigate the taste of incipient rot and stabilize the mixture, they added handfuls of pure saltpeter, extracted directly from the structure's tectonic faults, where the salt crystallized over the metal like a corrosive frost. The steam emanating from the cauldrons had a thick density, heavy with mineral particles and the pungent aroma of ammonia, a combination that impregnated the clothes and lungs of those waiting in line, constantly reminding them that their survival depended on the ingestion of elements designed for other purposes.

Marbo remained on the periphery of the cooking area, his eyes scanning the viscosity of the mixture with the precision of a systems auditor. In his mind, the process translated into a series of thermodynamic equations: he memorized the exact caloric density per portion, calculating how much kinetic energy could be derived from that grayish, saline mush. He knew that the mixture, under the atmospheric pressure of the stratum, would turn into a solid and highly storable block, capable of withstanding weeks of extreme humidity without developing biological mold that was not, in itself, part of the main ingredient. For him, the food held no moral or nutritional value in the traditional biological sense, but was rather a logistical requirement, a fuel load necessary to maintain the operation of the refugees' organic engines within the operational parameters of the march.

Lein, with automatic movements devoid of any trace of warmth, began to distribute the splintered wooden bowls among the crowd of refugees huddling under the industrial canvas. Each ration was a portion of thick mud that adhered to the bowl with an unnatural tenacity. Upon tasting the first bite, the biological rejection was instantaneous and widespread; the civilians, whose digestive systems were unaccustomed to the levels of saltpeter or the clay-like texture of the processed moss, began to emit retching sounds. The taste, a mixture of rusted metal, concentrated mineral salts, and decomposing fats, triggered a spasm of collective nausea that swept through the tents—a murmur of retching and muffled protests lost in the echo of the engines that, far above their heads, continued their indifferent, cyclical hum.

The tragedy of biology manifested with clinical speed when an old man, sitting next to one of the load-bearing supports, collapsed over his own bowl. His digestive system, unable to process the osmotic shock caused by the thick saltpeter, entered a state of acute failure. Lein moved swiftly to his side, his hands feeling the hardness of the old man's abdominal tissue. Upon contact, Lein's belly reacted out of sympathy or a reflex response to the presence of the toxin, contracting in a spasm of violent cramps. His abdominal musculature tightened to the point of pain, a physical warning of the ration's instability; the body, in Tairra, always knew when the architecture of its fuel was failing, even if the will tried to ignore the alarm signal.

A few meters away, Dastael consumed his ration with an indifference that bordered on the spectral. His neurological trauma, a relic of his years of service in tactical demolition units, had eroded his ability to perceive taste or smell at conventional levels. While the others writhed or attempted to hide their discomfort, he chewed the clay-like mass with the same dispassionate efficiency with which a machine crushes gravel. To Dastael, that was not food, but an inert supply that had to be processed to prevent the organism's wear, one more component in the maintenance of his existence as a combat tool. His jaw, tense and rhythmic, shattered the clumps of moss and salt, without his face reflecting even a hint of aversion; he was the perfect model of adaptation to misery, a creature reduced to its primary function.

In a secluded corner, covered by the shadows cast by an oil-stained tarp, Bashole was occupied with his own maintenance. His fingers, long and scarred by thermal burns, cleaned the remains of organic fiber and sawdust that had become trapped in the grooves of his armor-piercing arrows. He used a piece of twine soaked in synthetic lubricant, a task he performed with obsessive care despite his hands trembling with a rhythmic frequency, a chronic symptom of central nervous system fatigue after months of exposure to fluctuating pressure. The twine squeaked against the metal of the arrow, a dry sound that overlapped with the bustle of the sick and the dying.

Despite the profound biological rejection and the cramps that began to claim several members of the march, the ration fulfilled its logistical objective with mathematical coldness. The displaced, aware that any interruption in the supply of calories would mean definitive abandonment in the slag corridors, began to swallow the bitter paste in a sepulchral silence. They did not seek pleasure or nutritional sustenance in the organic sense of the term; they sought the continuation of the pulse, the maintenance of blood pressure, and the persistence of consciousness. In that green, salty mud, they found the only fuel capable of keeping their hearts beating within the immense macrocosmic terrarium of Tairra, accepting that, to keep operating, they themselves had to become the weakest link in the industrial production chain.

capitulos.sinopsis: The use of distilled sludge briquettes generates a thermal signal that gives away the contingent's position.
texto: The atmosphere within the containment sector plummeted with geometric precision, descending through thermal gradients until it settled far below the threshold of biological freezing. The air, saturated with suspended metallic particles and synthetic moisture, began to crystallize in the interstices of the structure, turning every iron surface into an abrasive frost trap. The military scholars, men whose bodies were little more than an assembly of poorly fitted prosthetics and scar tissue, moved among the cargo carts with the methodical urgency of those who know that entropy admits no delays. With hand-held hydraulic levers, they forced open the sealed compartments, extracting rectangular blocks of distilled sludge briquettes, a substance compressed from the sediments that accumulated in the subsurface sumps.

Each briquette emanated a heavy aroma of industrial waste and oblivion, a dense, dark mass that felt to the touch like an amalgam of organic refuse and heavy machinery byproducts. Beneath the heavy-duty tarp of the main tent, the debate erupted with the coldness of a systemic failure. Marbo, whose left hand was a stump covered by a protective leather guard, gestured over the cargo blueprint with an intensity that barely masked the desperation of his internal logic. The officers, shadows hunched under the weight of their field gear, listened with clenched jaws, knowing that every decision was a survival algorithm where the primary variable always trended toward the depletion of resources.

Marbo's thesis was irrefutable, a technical judgment based on the material's chemical composition: distilled sludge, being a receptacle for the Oily Rain that perpetually filtered down from the upper levels, possessed a dangerously high concentration of volatile hydrocarbons. This was not conventional fuel, but a concentrated carbon soup that, upon reaching ignition point, would transform the shelter into a kinetic lighthouse within the infrared spectrum. He explained, in a rasping voice that sounded like the friction of rusted metal, that the heat would not be a simple thermal relief, but a radiant beacon that would pierce the sector's haze, revealing their position to any sensor or entity patrolling the crevices of Tairra.

The cold, however, possessed an authority that no military logic could contradict. The sound of children crying, a sharp, rhythmic wail reminiscent of steam pipe relief valves, began to seep through the layers of the refugees' tattered clothing. It was a frequency that shattered the group's cohesion; the soldiers, whose action protocols were usually impeccable, faltered under the pressure of that biological anguish. Facing the imminent collapse of the contingent's morale, Marbo fell silent, allowing the physics of necessity to overcome the physics of caution. With an almost imperceptible nod, he authorized the ignition of the briquettes—a death sentence disguised as charity.

A soldier brutally struck an iron link against the rough surface of a briquette, throwing sparks that embedded themselves into the compressed sludge. The material did not burn with the clean warmth of a campfire, but began to sweat heavy oils before succumbing to a lazy combustion. The flame, of a dirty, violet hue, flickered with the instability of an agonizing specter, expelling plumes of black smoke that behaved almost like a solid substance. The air inside the tent became saturated immediately, charged with an acrid, piercing stench of burnt hydrocarbon that, in seconds, turned into a toxic layer that forced the refugees to cover their faces, coughing against the oppression of the smoke.

The interior atmosphere, previously frigid and clean, transformed into a cauldron of reduced visibility where men's eyes shed black tears from the irritation. The smoke, unable to dissipate due to the impermeability of the tarp, swirled overhead, while the heat, though insufficient to warm numbed limbs, began to radiate from the center of the circle. The soldiers tried to protect the children by wrapping them in insulating blankets, but the fouled atmosphere had already penetrated their lungs, marking them with the same toxic imprint that defined the inhabitants of the deepest strata of Tairra—those who breathed the vapors of the eternal machinery.

Beyond the perimeter of the tent, in the hostile exterior where the gray fog of the sector drifted like a stagnant tide, the phenomenon became patent with geometric clarity. The columns of black smoke, dense and laden with particles, ascended rigidly toward the metallic Ceiling of the megastructure, violating the homogeneity of the environment with a thermal signal that projected into the visual spectrum like an electromagnetic pulse. This was no natural fire; it was an energy signature so specific and unnatural that any proximity sensor, even the most rudimentary of Tairra’s automated surveillance systems, would have categorized it as a high-priority anomaly.

Bashole, stationed at a lookout post erected atop a jumble of support beams, watched the rise of the smoke with the immobility of an iron statue. His optical system, calibrated to detect minimal movements in the hydrocarbon haze, suddenly caught an irregularity in the black swamp that stretched out before them. Asymmetrical figures, devoid of defined human contours, began to emerge from the silence of the fog. They moved with an erratic cadence, driven not by muscles, but by the magnetic attraction that the heat of the bonfire exerted on their internal configuration, advancing with a determination lacking any will of its own.

They were the Noise-Crystallized, entities whose structure was composed of metallic debris and the static of the terrarium. As they neared the radius of the thermal radiation's influence, their surfaces began to flicker with horizontal flashes of crimson red, an involuntary response to the stimulus of the fire that drew them like moths to a reactor. Bashole recognized the threat immediately: the corrupted immune system of the megastructure had detected the foreign body, the heat source that defied the thermal equilibrium of the sector, and was sending its executors to purge the anomaly. The air vibrated with the screech of the gears that powered those beings, a sound that presaged the end of the tactical pause.

With the urgency of a forced gear, Bashole sounded the alarm horn, a piece of resonant metal that emitted a discordant note, cutting through the air like an industrial knife. Inside the tents, the reaction was instantaneous and desperate. The soldiers, abandoning all pretense of warmth, pounced on the bonfires with handfuls of wet mud and debris, smothering the flames with calculated violence. The smoke stopped abruptly, but the thermal mark had already been registered by the surrounding architecture. Darkness returned, heavy and frigid, wrapping the camp in an absolute silence where only the gasping breath of the refugees could be heard, while outside, the red static of the Crystallized advanced relentlessly over the metallic snow, searching for a heat signature that had already been extinguished, but whose trace persisted in the logic of the steel.

capitulos.titulo: Chapter 10: The Fatigue of the Tank
capitulos.sinopsis: Dastael suffers severe structural tears during the defense and Lein stabilizes his joints.
texto: The northern perimeter of the camp was not assaulted by living beings, but by an onslaught of pure entropy embodied in the Crystallized of Noise. Their cast-iron chassis, covered by mineral incrustations that resonated with Tairra’s vibratory frequency, struck the supply wagons with devastating geometric precision. The yew wood, chosen for its density and absorption capacity, succumbed into smoldering splinters under the dead weight of the attackers, whose limbs were pneumatic pistons loaded with the inertia of decades of industrial pressure. The din was an explosion of metal against wood, a symphony of fracture that alerted the vanguard that the sector’s integrity was being challenged by the inanimate.

Dastael stood over the breach like a pillar of steel in a world of wavering flesh. His position in the center of the collapsed iron bridge was a declaration of principles: where the structural failure threatened to devour everything it touched, he became the only stable point of support. With a kinetic movement that defied the fatigue of his own organic components, he brandished his two-handed greatsword, a weapon whose blade, notched by use but sharpened by necessity, cut through the air with a sharp whistle. Each blow connected with the precision of a surgeon, severing the crystalline limbs of the attackers who, in their mechanical blindness, tried to force their way through the warrior's body.

Dastael's defense was rhythmic, a sequence of slashes and blocks that followed the implacable logic of Tairra’s physics. Shards of crystal fell into the abyssal void that opened beneath the bridge, turning into fine dust before touching the lower thermal plates. He did not fight with fury, but with the mechanical efficiency of a component that knows its purpose and its limit. The brute force he employed in each arc of his sword did not stem from emotional will, but from an architecture of tendons and bones that began to complain under the stress of the fray, emitting subtle sounds of tension that only an ear trained in the mechanics of the strata could detect amidst the chaos of combat.

At the climax of the onslaught, a massive construct, an amalgam of foundation iron and hydraulic shafts, rammed Dastael’s shield with a mace that bore the mass of a support beam. The impact reverberated through the entire structure of the bridge, displacing the metal plates beneath both combatants' feet. The shield, designed to absorb frontal impacts, gave way just enough for the kinetic force to be transferred directly to Dastael's shoulder. The joints of his armor, composed of shape-memory alloys, fractured with a dry snap, while the warrior was pushed several centimeters backward, though without losing his verticality.

Dastael, however, remained impassive. His nervous system, conditioned by years of exposure to the electromagnetic fields of the upper levels, was under the effects of chemically induced analgesia. The physical pain, which should have paralyzed his limbs after the shoulder trauma, was a null variable in his biological processor. He did not feel the fissure in his structure, nor the scorching heat that began to emanate from the friction between his bone and the damaged armor. He continued his defense with supernatural rigidity, executing the choreography of war as if his own biology were an interchangeable spare part.

From the rearguard, Marbo observed the confrontation with an analytical coldness that bordered on dehumanization. His eyes, adapted to reading energy flows and mechanical tensions, detected an anomaly in Dastael’s movement cycle. Each time the warrior shifted his weight onto his right leg, a dull creak occurred, a discordant frequency indicating the imminent collapse of the joint tensors. Marbo’s memory maps, which contained the detailed anatomical blueprint of each member of the group, processed the data immediately: Dastael’s connective tissue was being overexerted beyond its fatigue limit, initiating a structural tear that would soon render the soldier unusable.

Once the battle ended, and the last fragment of the Crystallized of Noise plummeted into the void, the silence on the bridge became oppressive. Marbo approached Dastael and observed that, even though the combat had ceased, the warrior remained in a posture of constant alert, his legs as rigid as support cylinders. Upon inspecting the right ankle, Marbo’s conclusion was devastating: three of the main tendons had detached from their bony anchor, turned into useless fibers hanging within the skin. Dastael, however, ignored the gravity of the injury; to him, the body was a machine that kept functioning as long as there was no explicit shutdown command.

The urgent call was sent to Lein, who appeared in the dim light of the tent with medical repair instruments already laid out on a metal table. She did not ask about the origin of the damage; the state of Dastael’s tendons was an evident chronicle of the battle. As Lein knelt before the warrior to expose the bleeding joint, a somatic phenomenon occurred involuntarily: upon coming into contact with the torn tissue, Lein’s mechanical empathy—a side effect of her own nervous architecture altered by Tairra—caused a reflex tear in her own leg. The pain, sudden and sharp, wrung an involuntary moan from her, forcing her to close her eyes against the echo of a wound that was not hers.

Despite the copied agony coursing through her nervous system, Lein maintained absolute, almost inhuman concentration. The dimness of the tent, lit only by the flickering light of an unstable energy cell, did not prevent her from working with the precision of one stitching circuits instead of muscle. The cold sweat beading on her forehead mixed with the mud covering Dastael’s boots, forming a grayish paste on the floor. She stabilized the tendons, anchoring them back to the bony structure with high-resistance synthetic fiber threads, all while feeling her own body’s nerves attempting to process damage that refused to disappear.

Dastael watched the process without blinking, keeping his greatsword resting on his shoulder as if it were an extension of his own spine. There was no trace of fear or doubt in his gaze; he was an observer of his own repair, aware that he depended on Lein’s skill to once again be the group’s unbreakable shield. The bond between them solidified in that moment of shared silence: she acted as his sensory nerves, bearing the weight of the pain so that he could continue to be the weapon of resistance. In Tairra, survival was not a matter of individual integrity, but the sum of the parts that held each other up in the midst of the metal’s fatigue.

capitulos.sinopsis: Bashole suffers from a peripheral nervous breakdown and Marbo doses his opium extract to save the bow.
texto: The encampment in the fault sector was not a sanctuary, but an open wound in the floor of Tairra, a confined space where the air, laden with pulverized iron particles and the lingering scent of burnt ozone, clung to the mucous membranes like a layer of metallic frost. Following the recent skirmish, the calm that descended upon the survivors held the texture of a suspended threat, a tense silence where every breath felt like a lack of respect toward the vigilance demanded by the megastructure. The men and women, shadows worn thin by rust and fatigue, moved with the slowness of defective automatons, conscious that any sudden movement in the gloom could trigger a pressure sensor or unleash a collapse in the sector's integrity.

Bashole, whose hands had maintained lethal precision during the exchange of projectiles, crawled toward his assigned corner behind the supply tarp, a space bounded by rusted spare-parts crates and fragments of coaxial cabling. The cessation of adrenaline, far from bringing relief, marked the onset of a relentless chemical reconfiguration within his organism; as he abandoned the state of maximum alert, his autonomic system began to suffer a peripheral short circuit, a delayed response to the extreme tension imposed upon his muscles during the battle. Bashole's biology, worn down by years of hyper-control, rebelled against the passivity, turning his extremities into instruments of indomitable neurological chaos.

His fingers, usually capable of holding the weight of death with the delicacy of a feather, began to vibrate at an erratic frequency that defied his conscious will. He attempted to raise a bowl of weathered wood to his lips, filled with a paste of processed moss whose density was the only thing maintaining his metabolic equilibrium, but the effort was futile. The tremor, a high-frequency oscillation that traveled from his shoulders down to his phalanges, turned the act of eating into a technical impossibility; the bowl, unable to withstand the uncontrolled vibration of his hands, slipped between his fingers with a dull thud.

The impact against the iron floor was dry and blunt, causing the green mud, rich in synthetic nutrients, to scatter in a shapeless stain that marred the industrial neatness of the zone. Bashole observed the disaster with a gaze devoid of emotion, an existential vacuum that hid a calculating frustration: that spilling was not just lost food, but physical evidence that his most valuable tool, his own motor stability, was beginning to disintegrate under the weight of his personal history. The silence that followed the sound of the bowl was the only verdict that mattered in Tairra, where the waste of resources was paid for with the currency of one's own survival.

This deterioration was not a product of chance, but the direct result of a life consecrated to the architecture of stillness. To achieve the precision necessary in the arc of Tairra, Bashole had subjected his nervous system to a pathological training, freezing his breath for entire cycles and containing the blood flow in his limbs to avoid the micro-oscillation of the arteries. That constant forcing of human physiology had left an indelible mark: chronic damage to the peripheral nerves that now, in repose, manifested as an unbearable betrayal. Without the capacity to maintain a steady pulse, Bashole was, in the eyes of the megastructure, a useless part destined for the scrap heap.

He gritted his teeth until the enamel protested, his jaw locked in a grimace of denial at the weakness taking hold of his body.
